question_text: A student draws a free body diagram for a 5 kg object being pulled by a rope with 25 N tension at an angle. The object experiences 10 N of friction opposing motion. After calculating the resultant force is 15 N, what is the acceleration? Also explain why knowing the resultant force is essential before using F = ma.
correct_answer: Acceleration = 3 m/s². The resultant force must be calculated first because F = ma uses the NET force, not individual forces; using individual forces would give incorrect acceleration values.
explanation: Using a = F/m with resultant force: a = 15 N ÷ 5 kg = 3 m/s². The key insight is that Newton's second law applies to the resultant (net) force only—if you used the 25 N tension alone, you'd get the wrong answer. This is why free body diagrams and combining forces correctly are so important.
